burial.plot_notes: Between Sec 2, Site 3539 and Site 3536. Tombstone location not engraved; identified by adjacent engraved stones per FindAGrave.

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War colonel; fifty battles, no wounds

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Moses Crafts and Sarah Sturtevant Crafts of Monson Piscataquis County Maine. Both non-Hooker, not built. Per The Crafts Family genealogy 1630-1890.

education.school_name: Academies of Monson, Foxcroft, and Hebron
education.notes: Per Crafts family genealogy: 'educated at the academies of Monson, Foxcroft and Hebron, Me.'

career.notes: Pre-war career per Crafts family genealogy.

career.role: Collector of US Internal Revenue, 1st District of NY
career.organization: US Treasury Department / Internal Revenue Service
career.notes: Federal appointive office. Per Crafts family genealogy 1890.

military_service.rank: Private → 2nd Lieutenant → Colonel (brevet, for gallantry at Gettysburg) — Adjutant 102d NY Vol
military_service.notes: Enlisted private. 2nd Lt on staff of Maj. Gen. Franz Siegel during Shenandoah Valley. Commissioned Colonel for gallantry at Gettysburg, commanded skirmish line covering part of Gen. Slocum's corps with 150 men; held against rebel battle line until only 12 survivors retired with him. Adjutant 102d NY Vol. Per Crafts family genealogy: served throughout the war, took part in over 50 battles, was never taken prisoner, was never in the hospital, never received even a scratch, though bullets many times passed through his clothing and in some instances his hair. Battles documented in genealogy: Harper's Valley 1862, Shenandoah Valley, Chancellorsville, Winchester, Newtown, Cedar Creek, Culpepper Court House, White Sulphur Springs, Rappahannock, Manassas, Centerville, Fredericksburg, Gettysburg, Lookout Mountain, Missionary Ridge, Pea-vine Creek, Ringgold GA; 1864 under Sherman: Mile Creek Gap, Snake Creek Gap, Resaca (captured rebel artillery, regiment connected with Gen. Harrison's 70th Indiana Volunteers), Cassville, New Hope Church (Dallas), Acworth Bridge, Pine Mountain, Kennesaw, Atlanta.

narrative_blocks.header: Fifty Civil War battles, no wound — at Gettysburg
narrative_blocks.body: Crafts enlisted as a private in the 102nd New York Volunteers and rose to colonel for gallantry at Gettysburg, where he held the skirmish line with 150 men until only a dozen survived. Per the Crafts family genealogy, across over fifty battles from Chancellorsville to Atlanta he was never taken prisoner, never hospitalized, and never even scratched, though bullets passed through his clothing and hair.

narrative_blocks.header: Bullets passed through clothing and hair, never flesh
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Crafts family genealogy of 1890, he took part in over fifty Civil War battles. He was never taken prisoner, never hospitalized, and never received even a scratch — though 'bullets often passed through his clothing and hair.'

narrative_blocks.header: Commissioned Colonel for Gettysburg's last twelve men
narrative_blocks.body: At Gettysburg in July 1863 he held the skirmish line with 150 men of the 102nd New York Volunteers until only twelve survived. The Colonel's commission was awarded for that gallantry on the spot.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried beside his wife at Arlington National Cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: He died Christmas Day 1922 at Centereach Suffolk County New York at eighty-seven. He was buried at Arlington National Cemetery between his wife Lizzie Goodyear Crafts's plot in Section 2 Site 3539 and Site 3536 — his own stone unmarked but identified by the adjacent engraved markers.

narrative_blocks.header: Educated at Brooklyn Packard, Boston Conservatory of Music
narrative_blocks.body: May Crafts was born August 16, 1875, the elder of two children of Col. Francis Marion Crafts and Eliza Goodyear Crafts. She was educated at the Brooklyn Packard Institute and at the Boston Conservatory of Music.

burialCemetery.name: Arlington National Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: National military cemetery. Burial place of Dr. John Minor (HD2760), WW1 veteran. Absorbed duplicate CEM475 Stage 6Y-691 per compiler ruling; the nation’s preeminent military cemetery — every attached ID verified retained in the merge audit.
